title: Señoritis

jokes.text: Narrator introduces 'Perfecto Telles' as the boy Maeby is dating, and she rewards him for doing her English homework 'even though English was his second language.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is having Perfecto do her English homework
jokes.punchline: English is his second language — she's getting worse help than a native speaker would provide
jokes.explanation: Clean ironic setup delivered efficiently by the narrator. The absurdity of hiring a non-native speaker for English homework is a small but sharp observation that sets up Maeby's chaotic logic perfectly.

jokes.text: 'And if you do my Spanish, I'll teach you how to French.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is trading favors — Spanish homework for a lesson in something
jokes.punchline: 'French' — triple entendre: the French language, French kissing, and the comedic pivot from academic to romantic currency
jokes.explanation: One of the tightest wordplay jokes in the episode. The joke works on three simultaneous levels (language, kissing technique, transactional seduction), is perfectly economical, and is completely consistent with Maeby's scheming character. The setup is airtight and the punchline lands with no wasted syllables. Approaches 8+ territory because the construction is genuinely brilliant.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Everyone could see that they had chemistry together, but it wasn't until Donny Richter's algebra class that the real bonding began.'
jokes.setup: Reference to their romantic chemistry
jokes.punchline: Pivot to 'bonding' in an algebra class — a science/math wordplay that also foreshadows their academic struggles
jokes.explanation: Solid narrator wordplay — 'chemistry' and 'bonding' pivot from romantic to academic registers. Competent but not the sharpest pun in the episode.

jokes.text: Teacher tells struggling students to close their eyes, then those who passed may exit. A film crew member asks if that includes them. Teacher: 'No, the "Babies Having Babies" crew...'
jokes.setup: A classroom ritual to spare students' shame
jokes.punchline: A documentary film crew called 'Babies Having Babies' is embedded in this math class — their presence is so normalized even the teacher addresses them by name
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of a documentary crew with that name being a normal fixture in a high school class is a great world-building joke. Sets up a running gag and the show's self-referential meta layer.

jokes.text: Perfecto and Maeby reveal their failing test scores: '38.' '43, but that's because I'm good with numb— oh, no, actually it's 34.'
jokes.setup: Students reveal their failing grades, Perfecto tries to brag about his score
jokes.punchline: He talks himself into a lower score mid-sentence — he's not good with numbers at all
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed self-own. Perfecto's boast that he's 'good with numbers' is immediately demolished by the act of boasting it. The midsentence correction is perfectly timed and character-consistent.

jokes.text: Teacher: 'We don't say "dummy," and unfortunately you two alge-tards are going to need some special tutoring.'
jokes.setup: Teacher corrects a student for saying 'dummy'
jokes.punchline: Immediately uses 'alge-tards' — a portmanteau that's arguably worse than 'dummy' — as his own coinage for algebra students
jokes.explanation: A textbook irony joke executed with near-perfect economy. The teacher's self-contradiction is instant and the invented word 'alge-tards' is both funny on its own and expressive of his cluelessness. Strong enough to justify 8+ because the structure is flawless and the word itself is a genuinely novel coinage.

jokes.text: Students on the bus singing '99 Bottles of Beer' while headed to college tutoring.
jokes.setup: High school students are bused to a college for remedial tutoring
jokes.punchline: They're singing a drinking song on a school bus — infantile behavior underscoring their academic level
jokes.explanation: Functional background joke. The irony of a drinking song on a school bus of remedial students is mildly amusing but is the weakest kind of AD joke — mostly texture.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Are you kidding me? What kind of loser spends his free time tutoring high school kids?' Cut to George Michael.
jokes.setup: Maeby rhetorically asks what loser would tutor high school kids
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to George Michael, her cousin, as the revealed answer
jokes.explanation: Classic AD structure — rhetorical question answered by immediate visual cut. The economy is exceptional: Maeby's put-down lands, then George Michael's appearance gives her and us the answer simultaneously, adding a layer of cringe.

jokes.text: Hey, when we get there, don't forget you're Perfecto's woman. I don't want to hear about some smooth college guy who stole you away from me.
jokes.setup: Perfecto gives Maeby a jealous warning about college guys.
jokes.punchline: The audience knows — but Perfecto doesn't — that they're going to be tutored by George Michael Bluth, Maeby's cousin.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony here is exceptional — the audience is primed for the reveal of George Michael before it happens, making the setup land harder on rewatch. The "smooth college guy" description ironically describes the most awkward possible person. Character-specific irony at its best.

jokes.text: George Michael and Maeby recognize each other. He says 'I thought you were the girl. I'm supposed to tutor a kid from...' 'High school, yeah, that's me.'
jokes.setup: George Michael expected to tutor a younger high school student, not his adult cousin who he's quasi-dated
jokes.punchline: Maeby is the high school student — she's perpetually stuck in senior year
jokes.explanation: The cringe of cousins reuniting under these circumstances, with the added absurdity that she is genuinely still in high school, is well-executed. Rewatch bonus because the full joke requires knowing Maeby's arc.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Didn't we graduate together five years ago?' Narrator: 'It was a decent question.'
jokes.setup: George Michael asks the obvious question about Maeby still being in high school
jokes.punchline: The narrator validates his question with the driest possible affirmation — 'It was a decent question' — implying it was actually an excellent question that exposes Maeby's absurd life
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan understatement is a signature AD move, but 'It was a decent question' is particularly well-calibrated — modest praise that implies the question is devastatingly apt.

jokes.text: Narrator recaps that Michael told George Michael 'She might not be a blood relative, but she is still family' — recontextualizing why George Michael backed away from Maeby.
jokes.setup: George Michael is about to have The Talk with Maeby about their relationship
jokes.punchline: The reason he's ending things is his dad told him it's still incest-adjacent even if they're not blood relatives — played completely straight
jokes.explanation: This is a classic AD callback to the long-running cousin romance subplot. The joke is in the narrator's matter-of-fact retelling of deeply weird family logic.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Michael found the girl he now had to back away from on her back.'
jokes.setup: The narrator is describing George Michael finding Maeby in a compromising position
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'back away from on her back' — a subtle double meaning that implies she's lying on the floor while also gesturing at backing away from the relationship
jokes.explanation: Solid AD narrator wordplay. The double meaning is subtle enough to reward close listening but broad enough to register on first watch.

jokes.text: Maeby is lying on the floor to get her parents to notice her. She says she's been there 'for, like, an hour and a half.' Narrator: 'It had been seven minutes.'
jokes.setup: Maeby dramatically claims to have been lying on the floor for over an hour
jokes.punchline: The narrator punctures the drama: it's been seven minutes
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-fact-checker is one of AD's most reliable tools, and 'seven minutes' is the perfectly deflating specific number. Shows Maeby's flair for self-pity.

jokes.text: George Michael suggests: 'Do you think it would help if I went over and said, like, "Hey, Maeby's dead"?' Maeby: 'No, that would just mean that they notice you.'
jokes.setup: George Michael offers to alert Maeby's parents that she's 'dead' to get their attention
jokes.punchline: Maeby points out that would only make her parents notice George Michael, not her — a darkly funny indictment of how invisible she is to them
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully dark joke about neglectful parenting. Maeby's rebuttal is simultaneously the logical conclusion of the setup AND a devastating comment on her relationship with her parents. Both the setup and payoff are character-specific and the economy is excellent. Justifies 8+ because it earns its darkness through character truth.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Tobias are heard singing '♪ No, it's just a phallus... eee! ♪' offscreen while Maeby lies ignored on the floor.
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to get her parents to notice her by pretending to be ill
jokes.punchline: Her parents are audibly in a sexual situation involving the word 'phallus' — they're oblivious and distracted in the most inappropriate way possible
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of the Tobias/Lindsay relationship intersecting with Maeby's neglect plot is perfectly timed. The exclamatory 'eee!' at the end adds a physical comedy dimension.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Where's that from?' Maeby: 'It's from nothing.'
jokes.setup: George Michael hears the strange singing from Maeby's parents
jokes.punchline: Maeby's flat denial — 'It's from nothing' — is funnier than any explanation could be
jokes.explanation: Short, sharp, and perfectly deadpan. 'From nothing' is exactly what Maeby would say and exactly the reaction of a child used to her parents' weirdness.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Why don't you cough or something?' Maeby: 'Oh, right, like I'm dying.' Then she begins coughing dramatically while the song plays on. 'There's nothing keeping us together. I believe we're thinking the same thing.'
jokes.setup: George Michael advises Maeby to fake illness; she agrees, coughing dramatically
jokes.punchline: The background song lyric 'There's nothing keeping us together' plays as George Michael is about to discuss their relationship — double meaning as commentary on both the song and their cousin romance
jokes.explanation: The background lyric doing double duty as commentary on their relationship is a classic AD layering trick. Works better on rewatch.

jokes.text: George Michael is about to end things, Maeby thinks he wants to get more serious. Narrator reveals Maeby didn't want that — 'George Michael wasn't a very good kisser' — then launches into an extremely clinical anatomical description of his bad kissing technique.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains why Maeby isn't enthusiastic about the relationship getting more serious
jokes.punchline: A hilariously clinical, almost medical breakdown of George Michael's terrible kissing — 'laterally moved his lower jaw while constricting his lower lip... the upper lip tended to retract, revealing an exposed area from the front teeth to the canines, ultimately creating more pain than pleasure'
jokes.explanation: One of the standout jokes of the episode. The contrast between the emotional weight of the scene and the narrator's clinical dental-textbook language is genius. 'Creating more pain than pleasure' is a devastating punchline. The specificity ('front teeth to the canines') elevates it from a generic bad-kissing joke to a precise comic dissection. Clearly in 8+ territory — the structure is impeccable, the originality is high, and it rewards the audience's attention.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Really just rookie mistakes.'
jokes.setup: The narrator has just described an almost grotesque breakdown of George Michael's kissing failures
jokes.punchline: Dismissing all of it as 'rookie mistakes' — as if this is a trainable skill being graded professionally
jokes.explanation: The perfect coda to the clinical kissing analysis. 'Rookie mistakes' is condescending in exactly the right register. Extends the joke perfectly without overstaying its welcome.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'And that doesn't make me hotter to you?'
jokes.setup: George Michael says his dad doesn't want them to be together
jokes.punchline: Maeby's instinct is to weaponize parental disapproval as a source of attraction — textbook Maeby logic
jokes.explanation: Completely in character for Maeby, who frames everything as a transaction or leverage. The line's economy is excellent and it gets a laugh precisely because it's so Maeby.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'So first my parents don't notice that I drowned, and now my cousin doesn't care that I'm at the height of my sexuality.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is tallying her grievances after being rejected by George Michael and ignored by her parents
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'height of my sexuality' used as a complaint to a cousin is absurdly inappropriate in exactly the right way
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable. The juxtaposition of 'drowned' (neglect) with 'height of my sexuality' (entitlement) in one sentence is a remarkable compression of Maeby's character. The fact that she's saying this to her cousin makes it even funnier. Strong 8.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'We're a couple of, uh, forces of nature.' Maeby: 'What?' George Michael: 'Forces of nature.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to let Maeby down easy while agreeing she's attractive
jokes.punchline: He overcorrects into the baffling compliment 'forces of nature' — then doubles down when questioned
jokes.explanation: Classic George Michael awkwardness — the worse the situation gets, the more he doubles down on an inappropriate word choice. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'That's a great idea. I've got two whole months to flunk. That's got to disappoint them.'
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests Maeby try to graduate to get her parents' attention
jokes.punchline: Maeby immediately inverts his advice — she'll use the graduation timeline to strategically fail instead
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Maeby's scheming logic. She takes good advice and weaponizes it into a bad plan immediately. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Title card: 'It's Maeby's "Arrested Development."' — The episode title card parodies the show's own title.
jokes.setup: The standard AD season 4 episode structure gives each character their own title card
jokes.punchline: The title card both names the episode and references 'Arrested Development' as a condition (failing to mature/graduate) rather than just the show title
jokes.explanation: The show's own title being used as a literal descriptor of Maeby's failure to grow up is a clever meta-joke. 'Señoritis' as the episode title (a play on 'señorita' + 'senioritis') adds another layer.

jokes.text: Maeby's parents left town, so 'she put her plan to put her graduation on hold on hold.'
jokes.setup: Maeby planned to fail on purpose to disappoint her parents, but they left
jokes.punchline: The triple-negative construction 'put her plan to put her graduation on hold on hold' is a grammatical tangle that mirrors the absurdity of Maeby's life
jokes.explanation: The recursive 'on hold on hold' construction is a characteristically dense AD narrator joke. It requires a second of processing, which is part of the pleasure.

jokes.text: On the Gangie film set in India: 'Do we have to pay them in money or in rice?' 'If they're in the union, you have to pay with rice.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is supervising a film shoot in India with a massive crowd of extras
jokes.punchline: Union membership is what determines whether they get rice vs. money — an absurdist inversion of labor economics
jokes.explanation: The joke subverts labor union expectations by applying them to an entirely different currency. Absurdist but grounded in Hollywood satire.

jokes.text: Crew member: 'You'd think someone would tell her to shave her arms, the poor thing.' — referencing a woman in traditional Indian dress.
jokes.setup: Someone on the crew comments on a local woman.
jokes.punchline: The crew member applies Hollywood beauty standards to a local woman with zero self-awareness — the cruelty and obliviousness are the joke.
jokes.explanation: Functional Hollywood-culture satire about the crew's myopia. Works as a character comedy beat but isn't especially original.

jokes.text: 'Gangie on the Ganges? That doesn't even make sense.'
jokes.setup: They're filming 'Gangie IV' on the Ganges river in India
jokes.punchline: Someone points out the title 'Gangie on the Ganges' is nonsensical — but the franchise's logic was already absurd to begin with
jokes.explanation: A self-aware joke about the Gangie franchise's inherent ridiculousness. The callback to Maeby's long-running movie career earns this a slight bonus.

jokes.text: Hair and makeup question: 'Pretty makeup or scary makeup?' 'Scary.' 'I think we have the crew that did The Real Housewives of Beverly Hills.'
jokes.setup: They need scary makeup for the 'Gangie' horror film
jokes.punchline: The Real Housewives crew is presented as the natural horror makeup team — an insult to reality TV disguised as a practical solution
jokes.explanation: A solid celebrity/reality TV burn. The joke is the implication that Real Housewives' makeup is inherently scary — a mean but earned observation.

jokes.text: The shaman advising Lindsay: 'You have no children?' 'No. Why do you ask?' 'Well, yes, a daughter. She's away at boarding school in England.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is receiving spiritual advice; the shaman asks if she has children
jokes.punchline: Lindsay denies having children, then admits to a daughter — but has invented an elaborate false backstory (boarding school in England) to emotionally distance herself from Maeby
jokes.explanation: A devastating character beat for Lindsay. Her maternal denial is pitch-perfect and consistent with her arc. The 'boarding school in England' flourish shows she hasn't just forgotten Maeby but has actively constructed a fantasy.

jokes.text: 'Please don't squeeze the shaman.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is in a spiritual moment with a shaman who is offering a hug
jokes.punchline: The line is absurd on its face — 'don't squeeze the shaman' has no precedent as a social rule — and Lindsay's delivery implies she's been corrected on this before
jokes.explanation: A standalone absurdist gem. Four words, maximum comedy. The phrase 'squeeze the shaman' sounds wrong in every possible way and yet is perfectly logical in context. High quotability. One of the most memorable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Stop with the prayer hands. It just looks like you're out of ideas.'
jokes.setup: Someone is making prayer hands as part of the spiritual/India setting
jokes.punchline: The narrator reframes the gesture as a sign of desperation rather than spirituality
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational line from the narrator. Reframes prayer hands as performative helplessness rather than reverence — a cynical read that's genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Maeby tries to insult Lindsay: 'Don't you think it makes you seem a little, um, what is that saying, full of (bleep)?' Narrator: 'Thank you, Maeby, that's actually a compliment in India.' 'My own fault.'
jokes.setup: Maeby tries to use Lindsay's own insult against her
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the phrase is a compliment in India — her attack backfires; 'My own fault' is Maeby's instant self-aware admission
jokes.explanation: The callback to the 'full of bleep' motif pays off with a cultural twist. 'My own fault' is an unusually self-aware moment for Maeby and lands well.

jokes.text: Maeby re-enrolling for her senior year: 'Going back to school. Senior year!' No one in her family reacts. 'No? No one cares?'
jokes.setup: Maeby dramatically announces she's doing senior year again
jokes.punchline: Absolute silence — then her own breaking of the silence makes it worse
jokes.explanation: The awkward silence followed by Maeby's own commentary on it ('No? No one cares?') is a perfect encapsulation of her entire situation. The self-awareness makes it better.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'This is usually a tough time for teens. This is when drugs often become an issue.' Cut to: 'Time for Babies Having Babies!' 'Is "Babies Having Babies" on? That's my favorite show.'
jokes.setup: The narrator delivers a somber PSA-style warning about teens and drugs
jokes.punchline: The 'drugs' concern is immediately undercut by the family being excited about a TV show — and Tobias identifying 'Babies Having Babies' as his favorite show is its own character joke
jokes.explanation: The 'Babies Having Babies' running gag delivers here — Tobias calling it his favorite show is simultaneously an AD meta-joke (it's a show within the show that shadows Maeby's life) and a character beat.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Mother's Day Eve was the most joyous day of the year.'
jokes.setup: Standard scene-setting narration
jokes.punchline: 'Mother's Day Eve' — the family has invented a holiday that doesn't exist, treating it with the reverence of Christmas Eve
jokes.explanation: A throwaway line that's quietly brilliant. The Bluths celebrating 'Mother's Day Eve' with the same sincerity as a real holiday is perfectly absurdist. The confidence of the narration makes it land.

jokes.text: The family accidentally celebrated Thanksgiving a week early after 'they found that duck in the cupboard, tried to cook it alive, got scared, and then ended up eating it at a Chinese restaurant.'
jokes.setup: Narrator explains why the family celebrated Thanksgiving on the wrong day
jokes.punchline: The chain of events — found duck, tried to cook alive, got scared, Chinese restaurant — is an escalating absurdist disaster compressed into one sentence
jokes.explanation: This is a textbook AD escalation joke. The rapid-fire chain of absurd events in a single sentence (duck in cupboard → tried to cook alive → got scared → Chinese restaurant) has perfect rhythm and each beat is funnier than the last. The Chinese restaurant as a Thanksgiving fallback is the perfect deadpan landing. Firmly in 8+ territory.

jokes.text: Tobias leaving a voicemail for Maeby: 'Maeby, honey, Daddy has insisted on raising you alone.' Then Lindsay's message: '...Lindsay, I have met someone. It's not important who it is. Erase this message, and I'll leave her a mess...'
jokes.setup: Both parents are leaving competing voicemails for Maeby about their separation
jokes.punchline: Tobias's noble declaration 'Daddy has insisted on raising you alone' is immediately undercut by Lindsay's dismissive message — and Lindsay cuts herself off mid-word to prevent leaving any actual message for Maeby
jokes.explanation: The competing voicemails — both self-serving — are rich character comedy. Tobias's grandiosity vs. Lindsay's avoidance is a microcosm of their parenting styles.

jokes.text: Tobias's voicemail: 'Your daddy's not going to be around anymore. Excuse me, I swallowed a bug there. Uh, your daddy's not going to be around anymore.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is delivering what should be a dramatic, emotional message to his daughter
jokes.punchline: He interrupts his own devastating news to apologize for swallowing a bug — then simply repeats the line as if nothing happened
jokes.explanation: A great Tobias joke. The interruption of his own dramatic moment for something mundane (bug), then the exact repetition of the sad line, is perfect structure. The redelivery without any emotional adjustment is what makes it. Justifies 8+ — the structure is flawless and it's very Tobias.

jokes.text: The Widow Carr delivers foreclosure news and then casually adds: 'Also, Tobias, you got a callback for something called "The Big Bang Theory."'
jokes.setup: A woman arrives to deliver devastating news — the house is being foreclosed
jokes.punchline: She pivots without missing a beat to relay an acting callback for 'The Big Bang Theory' — a real show, implying Tobias was considered for a part on a successful show he would inevitably have blown
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash from foreclosure to acting callback is beautifully calibrated. Naming 'The Big Bang Theory' specifically (a massively successful CBS sitcom) makes the joke land harder because it implies Tobias could have been wealthy.

jokes.text: Guess what. I'll just tell you. / What? / 'Cause I feel like we'll be here forever, and I have a meeting. Um, you're fired.
jokes.setup: Studio executive appears to be building to an announcement.
jokes.punchline: She abandons the suspense entirely — "I'll just tell you" — and fires Maeby without ceremony.
jokes.explanation: The executive skipping her own dramatic buildup because she's in a hurry is a great beat — efficient cruelty. "I'll just tell you 'cause I feel like we'll be here forever" is a sharp corporate voice.

jokes.text: 'With no parents, no job, and no place to live, Maeby had squat, which is exactly what she decided to do... at her grandparents' penthouse.'
jokes.setup: Narrator describes Maeby's desperate situation
jokes.punchline: 'Squat' — used first as slang for 'nothing,' then literalized as the verb 'to squat (illegally occupy)'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best narrator wordplay moments. The pivot from 'had squat' (colloquial nothing) to the literal squatting action is seamless and satisfying. This is what AD's narrator does at its peak — the joke is invisible until the punchline lands.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And that's when Maeby did what we in the entertainment industry call "deadlining."'
jokes.setup: Maeby is squatting at the penthouse without paying
jokes.punchline: 'Deadlining' — a made-up industry term that sounds real, implying that occupying space without payment has a professional name in Hollywood
jokes.explanation: The invention of 'deadlining' as an entertainment industry term is a clever meta-joke. The narrator's 'we in the entertainment industry' adds a layer of complicity.

jokes.text: 'We're not moving in; we're occupying a space with central air.'
jokes.setup: Maeby justifies squatting at the penthouse
jokes.punchline: The specific addition of 'with central air' as if that changes the ethical calculus of illegal squatting
jokes.explanation: 'Central air' as the justifying amenity is a perfectly Maeby detail — she's not denying she's squatting, she's just noting the quality of the squat.

jokes.text: 'That is it — this is like living in 1999.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is forced to live in the model home with no internet
jokes.punchline: She describes the offline existence as the equivalent of living in 1999 — the last year before widespread internet made connectivity default
jokes.explanation: A sharply observational joke about millennial internet dependency. The specificity of '1999' as the cultural marker is well-chosen.

jokes.text: Maeby explains she's still in high school to get her parents to notice her: 'Let them think, "Oh no, should we be worried? Is she really that immature?"' George Michael: 'Yeah. Well, isn't it kind of a little immature to try and make your parents worry like that?' Maeby: 'Oh, that's good — I'm gonna try and work that in there.'
jokes.setup: George Michael points out the obvious irony that Maeby's plan is itself immature
jokes.punchline: Maeby agrees — but only as useful material to incorporate into her scheme, not as self-reflection
jokes.explanation: Maeby's instant reframing of George Michael's critique as material for her scheme rather than self-knowledge is perfectly in character. The joke captures her sociopathic scheming nature beautifully. 'I'm gonna try and work that in there' is one of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Michael shows up as George Michael's uncle; Maeby says 'Didn't say it was your cousin.' George Michael: 'Now, that's smart, let him think there's a girl in here.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at his son's dorm room
jokes.punchline: George Michael is actively hiding from his father that he has a female visitor — and his logic ('let him think there's a girl in here') is its own absurdity since Maeby IS a girl
jokes.explanation: The layer here is that George Michael is trying to seem normal to his father while hiding his cousin — and his cover story is accidentally accurate.

jokes.text: 'He's the worst. See, I think if George Michael and I didn't have each other as roommates, we'd be going crazy.'
jokes.setup: Michael is complaining about P-Hound as a roommate
jokes.punchline: Michael and George Michael living together was its own disaster — he's unaware he's the one who drove his son away
jokes.explanation: The irony lands for viewers who've seen Michael's episode — he's obliviously nostalgic for the very arrangement that made George Michael flee.

jokes.text: George Michael explains Fakeblock's technical details at length. 'It's just a Boolean-driven aggregation, really, of what programmers call "hacker-traps"...' Narrator notes Maeby's 'self-esteem plummeted as she started to question the entirety of what she had done with her life.'
jokes.setup: George Michael starts explaining his software using jargon
jokes.punchline: Maeby — who has spent years scheming and hustling in Hollywood — is humbled by her dorky cousin's technical competence
jokes.explanation: The irony of Maeby — the most successful teenage film producer in history — being humbled by computer jargon she doesn't understand is well-constructed. The narrator's clinical description of her emotional collapse is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You should see his Facebook page. Doesn't have a friend on it.' Narrator: 'Of course, that last part had nothing to do with Fakeblock... but it did help George Michael's self-esteem.'
jokes.setup: Michael brags about his son's privacy software, citing the empty Facebook as evidence
jokes.punchline: The narrator clarifies the empty Facebook is just because George Michael has no friends — not a feature of the software
jokes.explanation: The narrator's correction of Michael's accidental bragging is sharp. That George Michael gains self-esteem from being told his friendlessness is a feature (not a bug) is both sad and funny. Strong character specificity justifies the score.

jokes.text: On the bus back to high school, students ask if she wants to get high. Then: 'Can you imagine what kind of loser pretends to be in high school when they're in their 20s?' 'Well, it's pretty gutsy, right? I mean, it's better to take the risk, right?' 'Yeah, maybe you're right.' 'Yeah, maybe you're right.' 'Cop.'
jokes.setup: Students on the bus discuss what kind of loser would pretend to be in high school in their 20s — not knowing Maeby is exactly that
jokes.punchline: Maeby awkwardly agrees 'it's gutsy,' then they all immediately accuse each other of being a cop
jokes.explanation: A callback to the opening's 'what kind of loser' structure. Maeby now has to defend her own behavior to people who don't know she's that loser. The sequence ends with mutual 'cop' accusations in a great escalating paranoia beat.

jokes.text: Maeby at the bar asks for a Macallan 12 neat, water back. Bartender asks for ID. She says 'Can we make it — marry me — a Wild Turkey?' as if changing the order absolves her of ID requirements.
jokes.setup: Maeby orders a sophisticated whisky at a bar
jokes.punchline: When asked for ID, she pivots her order to Wild Turkey — as if requesting a cheaper whiskey would somehow fix her age problem
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur logic of changing the whisky order to avoid ID is absurdist and very Maeby — as if downgrading to a less sophisticated drink makes you less likely to be carded.

jokes.text: Perfecto: 'Well, not until his software hits and he can buy me an apartment. I'm living in a bleep-hole.'
jokes.setup: Perfecto asks if George Michael (the tutor) will steal Maeby away.
jokes.punchline: Maeby's nonchalant answer implies she's already planning to upgrade via George Michael's tech windfall — and Perfecto's complaint about his living situation reveals his class anxiety.
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat establishing Maeby's transactional view of relationships and Perfecto's situation. Not especially funny on its own but does setup work.

jokes.text: Bartender: 'I'm supposed to believe you're 17?' Maeby: 'Oh, no, sorry. That's my fake for sneaking back into high school. Here we go — 23.'
jokes.setup: Maeby shows fake ID to the bartender
jokes.punchline: She has two fake IDs — one to appear YOUNGER (to sneak into high school) and one to appear OLDER (to drink). She accidentally shows the wrong one.
jokes.explanation: The single best joke of the episode. It perfectly encapsulates Maeby's entire situation in one beat: she's so deep in her deceptions that she carries IDs in both directions simultaneously. The concept is wholly original, the structure is flawless, and it's 100% character-specific — no other character in any show could have this joke. The revelation (she has a fake to appear YOUNGER) is a genuine surprise that recontextualizes everything. This is what a 9+ looks like.

jokes.text: Bartender recognizes Maeby: 'Oh, you're Maeby. Well, you can have one, but you got quite a bar tab.'
jokes.setup: Maeby shows her real ID, the bartender recognizes her
jokes.punchline: She's a regular with a running tab — her adult identity at this bar is as established as her fake identity at school
jokes.explanation: A nice coda to joke #55. The bartender knowing her reinforces that her 'adult' life is as chaotic as her fake student life.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'George Michael, Kitty Sanchez.' Then Kitty: 'Drinks for the house.' 'This guy?' 'These guys are making a fortune off that "To Entrap a Local Predator" show they're on.' 'Were. Won't let us shoot in Laguna.'
jokes.setup: Maeby encounters old acquaintances at the bar
jokes.punchline: Kitty and George Michael are apparently starring in a 'To Catch a Predator' parody called 'To Entrap a Local Predator' — 'Were' because they've already been banned from Laguna
jokes.explanation: The callback to Kitty and George Michael from earlier seasons, combined with the very specific 'To Entrap a Local Predator' show title, is a dense AD joke. 'Were. Won't let us shoot in Laguna' is a beautifully deadpan extension.

jokes.text: Maeby notices Perfecto talking to someone at the bar: 'So, you fitting in over there at the high school? They accepting you?' Maeby: 'He's a cop.'
jokes.setup: An older man is asking Perfecto if he's 'fitting in' at high school
jokes.punchline: Maeby instantly clocks Perfecto as an undercover cop from this exchange — played as a visual/reaction beat
jokes.explanation: The irony of Maeby — the adult pretending to be a teenager — instantly identifying another adult pretending to be a teenager is sharp. Sets up the episode's third act well.

jokes.text: Are you guys looking for a new bait house? / And that's how she made enough money to at least pay for her bar tab.
jokes.setup: Maeby hears the "To Entrap a Local Predator" crew needs a new filming location.
jokes.punchline: Her instantly pivoting to offering a "bait house" — and the narrator confirming this paid off exactly one bar tab — is a perfect scale joke.
jokes.explanation: The comedic scale — her deal covers exactly a bar tab, not a meaningful financial win — is great understatement. "Bait house" being offered by Maeby with no apparent moral hesitation is peak character. The narrator's flat confirmation is perfect.

jokes.text: Barry the lawyer: 'After 21, it is illegal for you to enroll in high school in the state of California. As a matter of fact, you cannot even lurk.' 'What, do they got cameras in the bushes? Why would they do that? You can't even see into the locker rooms from there.'
jokes.setup: Barry explains the law about adults in high schools
jokes.punchline: Barry's knowledge of exactly where cameras are (and aren't) positioned relative to the locker rooms — revealing he's been lurking himself
jokes.explanation: Barry's self-incriminating knowledge of locker room camera blind spots is a sublime dark comedy moment. The joke reveals everything about his character in one beat. The structure is perfect: he sets up his own punchline by complaining about the cameras before realizing what he's said. Firmly 8+ — this is the kind of joke AD does better than any other show.

jokes.text: Barry? / Maeby, you scared the be-(bleep) out of me. What are you doing here? / Well, I could ask you the same thing. / I go to high school here. / I could answer the same thing.
jokes.setup: Maeby catches Barry — the family's incompetent lawyer — lurking at her high school.
jokes.punchline: Both of them are in high school for disreputable reasons; the parallel structure of their identical admissions is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The symmetry of Maeby and Barry both being adults illegally in a high school — and the perfect parallel structure of the dialogue — is excellent. Barry's "I could answer the same thing" is a great reveal through implication. High character integration for a recurring character.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Oh, well, that's easy. You get something incriminating on him.' Maeby: 'Yeah, well, maybe I'll just keep a record of it, too, so I can follow up with my own bill.' Barry: 'Oh, very good. You should be the lawyer. Very good. Pro bono.'
jokes.setup: Barry offers to bill her for his advice on how to blackmail Perfecto
jokes.punchline: Maeby counters she'll keep records to bill him back; Barry's response — 'You should be the lawyer. Pro bono' — is simultaneously a compliment and a concession that he won't be charging
jokes.explanation: Barry's legal incompetence meeting Maeby's scheming is a fun match. 'Pro bono' as his concession is the right landing.

jokes.text: What, do they got cameras in the bushes? Why would they do that? You can't even see into the locker rooms from there.
jokes.setup: Maeby asks if the school has cameras to catch lurkers.
jokes.punchline: Barry's objection to cameras in the bushes reveals exactly why he's been in the bushes — and that he knows the locker room sightlines.
jokes.explanation: One of the darkest and best-crafted jokes in the episode. The joke works on multiple levels: Barry's specific knowledge of camera placement and locker room visibility is a devastating self-own delivered completely straight. "You can't even see into the locker rooms from there" as a complaint (implying he's tried) is the perfect punchline. Dark, specific, character-perfect, and perfectly structured. Justifies 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Maeby runs into an ostrich in heat at the penthouse. 'And she wouldn't even let me get a cat.'
jokes.setup: Maeby goes to check the mail at the penthouse
jokes.punchline: An ostrich in heat is roaming the penthouse — played as just another obstacle in Maeby's day — and the 'she wouldn't even let me get a cat' line implies this is comparatively worse
jokes.explanation: The ostrich callback is a signature AD deep-cut. The 'wouldn't even let me get a cat' line implies the ostrich is worse than a cat, which is the right absurdist logic. Visual gag requires knowing the ostrich's Season 4 history.

jokes.text: Maeby finds a check from 'Gangie for facelift — Mother.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is searching for a long-overdue royalty check
jokes.punchline: The check exists but was taken by her mother Lindsay for a facelift — a check from the Gangie (Gangie IV: Face Lift) franchise was literally used for a face lift
jokes.explanation: A beautiful piece of AD wordplay. The subtitle of the movie ('Face Lift') is literally what Lindsay spent the royalty check on. The callback is seamless and the irony is earned. This is exactly the kind of layered joke the show is famous for. Strong 8+.

jokes.text: Check made out 'From Gangie for facelift' — 'Mother.'
jokes.setup: Maeby finds a check that was meant for her.
jokes.punchline: The check memo says 'from Gangie for facelift' — her royalty from the horror franchise was routed through her grandmother and earmarked for her mother's cosmetic surgery.
jokes.explanation: Dense AD information delivery — in one check memo line, we learn where the money went and why. The 'Mother.' as a standalone sentence is the right punchline beat.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I'm getting an Opie?' Narrator: 'The only bigger honor would be having an award like that named after you, I guess.'
jokes.setup: Maeby discovers she's receiving a lifetime achievement award for young people
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'I guess' undercuts the honor — suggesting even eternal fame as an award namesake would be 'I guess' impressive
jokes.explanation: The narrator's casual 'I guess' is mildly funny but not a standout. Sets up the Opie subplot more than it delivers comedy.

jokes.text: Teacher Donny Richter: 'What is it going to take to get you to focus? Are you on drugs? Because, from what I hear, there might be an undercover cop at this school.'
jokes.setup: A teacher tries to motivate Maeby to study
jokes.punchline: He warns her about the undercover cop as if that information is relevant to her academic performance — revealing he's either paranoid or personally worried about the cop
jokes.explanation: The teacher's unsolicited cop warning signals he has his own reasons to fear the undercover officer — a nice secondary comedy layer that pays off in the final reveal.

jokes.text: 'You know what? I think I will do better once I get a little Mexican in me.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is being pressured to focus on studying; she's been getting taco from the teacher; she has a plan to get close to Perfecto (who is Mexican)
jokes.punchline: The phrase works simultaneously as a food reference (Mexican food the teacher brought), a racial/cultural reference to Perfecto, and a sexual innuendo — all three meanings active at once
jokes.explanation: A triple-meaning line that operates on food, ethnicity, and sexual innuendo simultaneously. Only works in context (tacos on the desk, Mexican boyfriend, plan to seduce him). The economy — ten words, three active meanings — is exceptional. One of the episode's most quotable lines and a strong 8+.

jokes.text: Well, I'm going to go mingle with a bunch of 13-year-olds who think they can still play fifth grade. Have fun.
jokes.setup: George Michael drops Maeby at the Opie awards.
jokes.punchline: His description of the child entertainment award recipients — "13-year-olds who think they can still play fifth grade" — is unexpectedly cutting and adult.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's sudden cynicism about child actors is a funny character moment — he's usually earnest and kind. The specific detail of kids too old to play their typical roles is a sharp industry observation.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Maeby got her cousin to drive her to the Opies so she could impress him with her achievements.' Cut to: 'Well, I'm going to go mingle with a bunch of 13-year-olds who think they can still play fifth grade.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is going to an awards show for young entertainers
jokes.punchline: Her dismissive characterization of the attendees ('13-year-olds who think they can still play fifth grade') is ironic given she's 23 playing a 17-year-old
jokes.explanation: Maeby's contempt for child actors at a children's award show is perfectly self-unaware given her situation. The specific detail of 'fifth grade' adds precision.

jokes.text: Mort Meyers: 'They only give that award away to somebody after they die or, even worse, after your career is dead.' Maeby: 'Really?' Mort: 'Well, consider yourself lucky. They told you your career was over.'
jokes.setup: Mort is getting a lifetime achievement Opie
jokes.punchline: He explains the award only goes to people whose careers have died — then reframes it as Maeby being lucky she got the warning
jokes.explanation: 'Even worse, after your career is dead' and framing death-of-career as worse than actual death is a darkly funny Hollywood observation. 'Consider yourself lucky' is a well-structured inversion.

jokes.text: Mort: 'I passed on "Hunger Games." It wasn't the most graceful of exits... No, actually, the exit was pretty graceful. I think I just remember it that way 'cause it got dicey when I was sneaking back in to take a (bleep) on Sid's desk.'
jokes.setup: Mort describes getting fired as 'not the most graceful exit'
jokes.punchline: He corrects himself — the exit was graceful — but then reveals he snuck back in to defecate on the boss's desk, which was the dicey part
jokes.explanation: The mid-sentence correction followed by the escalating revelation about defecating on the desk is a textbook escalation joke with perfect structure. The casual delivery of the 'dicey' detail makes it funnier. 'That's the way the cookie crumbles' beforehand adds a layer of irony. Strong 8+.

jokes.text: Mort: 'When they kick you out of the business, save face by starting your own Internet company.' Maeby: 'You started an Internet company?' Mort: 'It's called Schnoodle.'
jokes.setup: Mort advises starting an internet company as a face-saving move
jokes.punchline: 'Schnoodle' — a nonsense word that sounds like a tech startup but is completely meaningless, delivered with full confidence
jokes.explanation: 'Schnoodle' as an internet company name is inspired absurdist naming. It sounds simultaneously like a tech startup (rhymes with 'Google,' 'Noodle') and a dog breed. Maximum comedy per syllable.

jokes.text: Maeby tells Rebel Alley she's started a company called Fakeblock. Rebel: 'What's this? You're talking about Fakeblock?' They discuss it. Mort chimes in: 'Well, if you need a name, I'm the guy who came up with Schnoodle.' Rebel: 'How do we know each other?' Mort: 'We don't. That's what it is.'
jokes.setup: Mort tries to insert himself into a conversation about Fakeblock by name-dropping his Schnoodle company
jokes.punchline: Rebel asks how they know each other; Mort's answer 'We don't. That's what it is.' — a non-answer delivered as if it explains everything — is pure absurdist confidence
jokes.explanation: 'We don't. That's what it is.' is a beautifully confident non-answer that implies knowing someone isn't necessary for Mort to claim relationship with them. Peak Mort Meyers character comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Fakeblock is just a software I'm developing. The whole point is that it's private. So you can't be telling people about it.' Maeby: 'No, we got to tell people about it. I just got a lifetime achievement award. I'm finished.'
jokes.setup: George Michael wants to keep Fakeblock private; Maeby wants to publicize it
jokes.punchline: Maeby's justification for going public — 'I just got a lifetime achievement award. I'm finished' — is self-aware career desperation deployed as an argument
jokes.explanation: The callback to Mort's 'career death' explanation of the award, now used by Maeby as leverage against George Michael, is well-structured. Her self-awareness about her career being 'finished' is darkly funny.

jokes.text: How does your privacy software work? / Huh? It's private.
jokes.setup: Rebel asks George Michael to explain how his privacy software works.
jokes.punchline: "It's private" — the perfect answer that is technically correct but wholly evasive, using the product's defining characteristic as a non-answer.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode — possibly the most purely constructed. The question "How does your privacy software work?" has only one possible answer that is simultaneously true, evasive, and hilarious: "It's private." Perfect logic, perfect economy, completely in character for both the product concept and George Michael's awkward genius. Highly quotable. Justifies the 9+ score — this is a genuinely exceptional joke construction.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Also, I think you should consider calling it "The" Fakeblock. It's cleaner.' George Michael: 'Like "The Netflick."'
jokes.setup: Maeby suggests rebranding by adding 'The' before the name
jokes.punchline: George Michael's example — 'Like "The Netflick"' — adds an 's' to make Netflix sound like a made-up word, parodying the 'The Facebook' era of branding
jokes.explanation: 'The Netflick' is a beautifully wrong version of Netflix — singular, with 'The,' echoing the early days of 'The Facebook.' The joke is smart about tech branding and the extra 'k' is a perfect touch. High quotability.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'What, you think they invite the animals and ask them to wait while they build the zoo? No, that would be a bloodbath.' George Michael: 'Yeah, a total (bleep) bloodbath.'
jokes.setup: George Michael says the software isn't ready to launch
jokes.punchline: Maeby's zoo metaphor — don't invite the animals until the zoo is built — and George Michael's enthusiastic agreement that it would be 'a total (bleep) bloodbath' extends it absurdly
jokes.explanation: The zoo metaphor is original and Maeby-specific (she thinks in Hollywood production terms). George Michael's enthusiastic 'bloodbath' agreement is a funny character beat — he's usually restrained but jumps on this one.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Come on, this could be my only chance to be a Harris.' George Michael: 'A Harris?' Maeby: 'All right, fine.' Narrator: 'Oh, heiress. You meant... You don't pronounce the "H."' Maeby: 'I pronounce the "H."' Narrator: 'No, but that's not how the word is...' Maeby: 'You know, we say words the way we say them for a reason.'
jokes.setup: Maeby mispronounces 'heiress' as 'Harris'
jokes.punchline: She doubles down on the mispronunciation, arguing linguistic relativity — 'we say words the way we say them for a reason' — a confident defense of her own error
jokes.explanation: A brilliant joke on multiple levels. 'Harris' as a mispronunciation of 'heiress' is already funny. The narrator correcting her (breaking the fourth wall to argue with a character) is meta. Her confident counter-argument about pronunciation is the best part — Maeby treats her own ignorance as a valid linguistic position. The narrator is left speechless. This is a 8.5 — exceptional character integration and originality.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'Is this another one of these social networking things? Because I am so over those. I tried to deactivate my Facebook profile. It's easier to get out of Scientology.'
jokes.setup: Rebel is being pitched on Fakeblock.
jokes.punchline: The Scientology comparison to Facebook deactivation — both are notoriously difficult to leave — is a sharp observation with celebrity-world specificity (Scientology) that lands in the context of an industry party.
jokes.explanation: Sharp line — the Scientology comparison is well-targeted for the Hollywood setting and the parallel is genuinely apt (both Facebook and Scientology make leaving procedurally difficult). Good economy, high quotability.

jokes.text: Rebel Alley: 'I tried to deactivate my Facebook profile. It's easier to get out of Scientology.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing the antisocial network Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Facebook is harder to leave than Scientology — a contemporary social media critique
jokes.explanation: A competent topical joke about Facebook's stickiness vs. Scientology. Well-delivered but not especially original — this joke had been made elsewhere by the time Season 4 aired.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'This is actually the opposite of Facebook. It's the antisocial network.' Rebel: 'It prevents piracy, right?' Maeby: 'Really?'
jokes.setup: Maeby explains Fakeblock as the 'antisocial network'
jokes.punchline: Rebel's non-sequitur 'it prevents piracy, right?' is completely wrong but Maeby's impressed 'Really?' suggests she's happy with any interpretation that sells the product
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Maeby's 'Really?' — she doesn't correct Rebel's wrong interpretation because any positive spin is useful. Shows her as a pure salesperson who cares nothing for accuracy.

jokes.text: George Michael gets the new name 'George Maharis.' Narrator: 'And that's how George Michael finally got that new name. It felt like a good fit — strong, rugged... untainted.' George Michael: 'Yes.' 'Yes, George Maharis.' 'Perfecto.' 'De nada.'
jokes.setup: George Michael needs a new name to distance himself from the Fakeblock connection
jokes.punchline: He lands on 'George Maharis'; Perfecto responds with 'Perfecto' (meaning both 'perfect' and his own name) and then 'De nada' — a three-layer bilingual pun
jokes.explanation: The 'Perfecto' response — working simultaneously as approval and as Perfecto saying his own name — is a beautiful construction. The narrator's 'untainted' as a descriptor of the new name (because the old name is tainted by family scandal) adds a dark layer. 'De nada' as the final beat is perfect economy. This is a genuinely clever multi-layer joke.

jokes.text: Maeby tries to entrap Perfecto as a predator: 'I may only be 17, Perfecto, but I know you're a cop.' Perfecto: 'Yeah, there it is, that cop stare.' Maeby: 'I got to tell you, I find it very sexy.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to seduce Perfecto into being a predator so she can blackmail him
jokes.punchline: She calls his bluff by saying she finds 'the cop stare' sexy — accidentally an accurate read since he actually is NOT a cop, making her seduction strategy doubly ironic
jokes.explanation: The cringe of Maeby's entrapment plan — and the irony that she's the one who's actually committing a crime — is well-layered. Rewatch bonus because knowing Perfecto's real identity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Perfecto confesses he's a cop hunting a rogue CIA operative named Esteves. 'It's the kids from "Modern Family."' 'Sorry. He's become a drug kingpin...'
jokes.setup: Perfecto is delivering dramatic exposition about the drug kingpin
jokes.punchline: George Michael interrupts with 'It's the kids from Modern Family' — a real show — breaking the tension completely; Perfecto says 'Sorry' and continues as if nothing happened
jokes.explanation: The show-within-a-show meta-joke (Modern Family existing in the AD universe, at an event full of young entertainers) is a classic AD touch. Perfecto's 'Sorry' and continuation adds a nice deadpan beat.

jokes.text: Look, I may only be 17, Perfecto, but I know you're a cop. / Yeah, there it is, that cop stare. / I got to tell you, I find it very sexy.
jokes.setup: Maeby attempts to seduce Perfecto by calling him out as a cop.
jokes.punchline: "I find it very sexy" — the aggressive flattery of calling someone's professional behavior a turn-on is an unusual seduction tactic.
jokes.explanation: A decent character beat — Maeby's manipulation style always involves aggressive directness. The "cop stare" being called out and immediately sexualized is appropriately Maeby.

jokes.text: After dramatic sequence: 'Rico!' 'Wow, you are good.' Perfecto: 'I have to be — young lives depend on it.'
jokes.setup: Perfecto is demonstrating cop bravado after a tense scene
jokes.punchline: 'Young lives depend on it' — delivered with dead seriousness by someone who is, we'll learn, just a high school senior with an anti-bullying badge
jokes.explanation: Perfecto's cop posturing is retroactively very funny when we discover his 'badge' is anti-bullying. 'Young lives depend on it' is a setup for his eventual reveal.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'She narrowly missed the $50,000 check with her name on it that her mother gave to her grandfather... who gave it to this man... who gave it to this man.'
jokes.setup: Maeby stops for coconut shrimp on the way to accept her award.
jokes.punchline: The check passes through multiple hands (grandfather, then others) in a chain of misappropriation — all because she paused for shrimp. The escalation of people the money passes through is set up like a visual gag (inferring we see each handoff).
jokes.explanation: Dense AD plotting where a single shrimp craving costs Maeby $50,000. The chain of transfers is a payoff of ongoing Season 4 plotting. Rewatch bonus for viewers tracking the money.

jokes.text: I tried. / Tell your therapist I tried. / I'm not in therapy! / Then I must have done something right.
jokes.setup: Lindsay attempts to connect with Maeby at the awards show.
jokes.punchline: After Lindsay says "tell your therapist I tried," Maeby says she's not in therapy. Lindsay interprets this as proof of her parenting success.
jokes.explanation: One of the best character jokes in the episode. Lindsay assumes Maeby is in therapy (because of course she would be, given the family) — then when Maeby denies it, Lindsay takes credit for her child NOT needing therapy as proof of good parenting. This is a deeply ironic reversal that works on multiple levels: Lindsay is an awful parent, Maeby probably should be in therapy, and Lindsay's self-congratulation is completely delusional. Perfect structure, perfect character integration, extremely quotable. Justifies 8+ in every dimension.

jokes.text: Mort at the Opies accepts his award: 'Thank you, Kirk Cameron, for that incredibly Bibley introduction.'
jokes.setup: Maeby (posing as Mort?) thanks Kirk Cameron for an introduction
jokes.punchline: 'Bibley' as an adjective for Kirk Cameron's introduction — a made-up word that perfectly characterizes his known Christian evangelism
jokes.explanation: 'Bibley' as an adjective is an inspired coinage — it says everything about Kirk Cameron's public persona in one syllable. The audacity of the joke (insulting a real person with a made-up adjective to their face) adds to the impact.

jokes.text: Maeby's Opie speech devolves into a profanity-filled declaration of starting Fakeblock: 'Tie yourself to your chair, because this is going to be a rough (bleep) ride. I'm leaving this (bleep) dying business to join the software game. Double (bleep) newsflash. I'm starting Fakeblock with George Maharis...' escalating through multiple 'go (bleep) yourself' statements, ending with 'Please welcome the talented voices of Phineas and Ferb.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is accepting a lifetime achievement award at a children's entertainment event
jokes.punchline: She turns the acceptance speech into a profanity-laced departure announcement, culminating in 'So you can all go (bleep) yourselves' — then a seamless cut back to the normal child-friendly programming ('Please welcome the talented voices of Phineas and Ferb')
jokes.explanation: The escalating speech is a great set piece. The tonal whiplash of profanity at a children's award show, then the instant pivot to 'the talented voices of Phineas and Ferb,' is perfectly structured. The Phineas and Ferb line is the ideal deadpan landing for the chaos.

jokes.text: Please welcome the talented voices of Phineas and Ferb.
jokes.setup: Maeby concludes her scorched-earth speech.
jokes.punchline: The show immediately pivots to introducing Phineas and Ferb — the complete tonal whiplash of returning to wholesome child entertainment after Maeby's rant is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash is the joke — the awards show's total non-reaction to the meltdown and immediate pivot to programming is perfect deadpan. The specific choice of Phineas and Ferb (a particularly wholesome animated children's show) maximizes the contrast.

jokes.text: Maeby to Lindsay: 'Are you really gonna whore yourself out like that?' Lindsay: 'I am not a whore.' Maeby: 'Yeah, you're a whore.'
jokes.setup: Maeby sees her mother cozying up to a political figure after an explosion
jokes.punchline: Maeby accuses Lindsay of whoring herself out; Lindsay denies it; Maeby simply confirms 'Yeah, you're a whore' — and the narrator reveals Maeby's objection is really about losing her income source
jokes.explanation: The exchange works on two levels — the apparent moral objection and the real mercenary reason. 'Yeah, you're a whore' as a simple restatement after the denial is deadpan and effective.

jokes.text: Campaign manager to Lindsay: 'Ma'am, is she yours?' Lindsay: 'Unfortunately.' Campaign manager: 'Herbert Love would like to see her.' Lindsay: 'You can arrange that?' Campaign manager: 'Obviously, discretion is appreciated.'
jokes.setup: A man approaches Lindsay after witnessing the 'whore' exchange and says Herbert Love wants to see Maeby
jokes.punchline: 'Discretion is appreciated' — the campaign manager is hiring Maeby as a prostitute, and Lindsay unknowingly facilitates it; the dark comedy is in how matter-of-fact the exchange is
jokes.explanation: The darkness of Lindsay accidentally becoming her daughter's pimp (while Maeby is actually operating the scheme) is a great AD recursive irony. 'Unfortunately' as Lindsay's response to 'is she yours?' is the right character beat.

jokes.text: And just to be clear, I want to hire her for prostitution. / Yes, no, definitely. That was clear. I'm gonna charge you for that.
jokes.setup: Campaign manager wants to confirm the nature of the transaction.
jokes.punchline: Maeby confirms she understood — then announces she'll bill him extra for the clarification.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Barry's billing joke (pro bono) but inverted — Maeby charging extra for confirming what was already clear — is perfectly Maeby. The matter-of-fact treatment of arranging her mother's prostitution as a business transaction is dark and funny. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Campaign Manager: 'And just to be clear, I want to hire her for prostitution.' Maeby: 'Yes, no, definitely. That was clear. I'm gonna charge you for that.'
jokes.setup: The campaign manager clarifies his intentions
jokes.punchline: Maeby's 'Yes, no, definitely. That was clear. I'm gonna charge you for that' — she's billing him for the clarification as if it were a legal service
jokes.explanation: Maeby charging for a prostitution clarification is perfectly absurdist and in character. 'Yes, no, definitely' is a great non-committal yes. The joke is both dark and funny because Maeby applies business logic to an illegal transaction. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Narrator: '...and became her mother's pimp.' Maeby approaching Lindsay: 'Of course, the plan required getting back into her mother's good graces to trick her into turning a trick.'
jokes.setup: Maeby needs Lindsay to meet Herbert Love
jokes.punchline: 'Trick her into turning a trick' — the wordplay of 'trick' (deception) into 'trick' (sex work) is dense and perfectly delivered
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best narrator lines. 'Trick her into turning a trick' is exceptional wordplay — both meanings of 'trick' active, delivered in six words. The economy is near-perfect and the dark humor is entirely earned through character. Strong 8.5.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'But this was a woman who once wore this to a men's prison, so she felt she'd be okay.'
jokes.setup: Maeby wonders if she can talk Lindsay into going along with the scheme
jokes.punchline: Reference to Lindsay's notorious prison visit outfit — a visual callback that characterizes Lindsay's oblivious sexuality as Maeby's trump card
jokes.explanation: A callback that works both for long-time viewers (who know the specific outfit) and newcomers (who can infer it was inappropriate). The narrator's confidence ('so she felt she'd be okay') is perfectly calibrated deadpan.

jokes.text: Jim Cramer on 'Mad Money': 'This Fakeblock thing is poised to explode! I never do this, hell, it's not even a stock yet. For all I know, it's not even real! But I think this might be going through the roof! I'm calling it my first "Hypothetical Buy."'
jokes.setup: Maeby has gotten Fakeblock mentioned on Mad Money
jokes.punchline: Jim Cramer invents a 'Hypothetical Buy' for a product that 'might not even be real' — a perfect parody of financial media hype culture, and an accidentally accurate description of Fakeblock's non-existence
jokes.explanation: The 'Hypothetical Buy' is a brilliant invention that satirizes both financial media hype and startup culture. Cramer's own disclaimer ('for all I know, it's not even real!') makes it doubly funny because Fakeblock literally isn't real. The cameo by the real Jim Cramer adds a meta layer. Strong 8+.

jokes.text: Jim Cramer: 'And this weekend, don't forget to catch me on "Gangie IV."' Then a clip of Gangie plays: 'This old lady's crazy! I'm raising the alert level from "Don't Leave the House" to "Board Up Your Windows!"'
jokes.setup: Cramer's Mad Money segment continues
jokes.punchline: Cramer has a cameo in Gangie IV; the Gangie alert levels ('Don't Leave the House' to 'Board Up Your Windows') are an escalating threat system from a horror movie about a grandmother
jokes.explanation: The Gangie terror alert levels are a great bit — parodying homeland security color-coded alerts for a movie about a scary old woman. Jim Cramer having a cameo in Gangie IV is another layer of AD absurdist worldbuilding.

jokes.text: Maeby coaches Lindsay on seducing Herbert Love: 'Guys who are used to being charged — I mean in charge — sometimes like it when the other person takes control.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is giving Lindsay dating advice
jokes.punchline: Maeby slips and says 'being charged' (for sex) before correcting to 'in charge' — a Freudian slip that reveals her real agenda
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect slip-of-the-tongue joke. The correction mid-sentence ('I mean in charge') is both in-character for Maeby (she catches herself) and exposes the scheme. The structure — setting up the correction as if it were a genuine mistake — is immaculate. The best of the episode's 8+ jokes alongside the fake ID bit and the kissing analysis. Clearly justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'What am I doing? This is disgusting. I've got money coming in.' — Her moral epiphany lasts exactly one sentence before being replaced by financial satisfaction.
jokes.setup: Maeby briefly has a moral qualm about pimping out her mother
jokes.punchline: Her 'disgust' is resolved not by ethics but by the reminder that she's making money — the qualm vanishes instantly
jokes.explanation: Maeby's pseudo-ethical moment resolved by money is a perfect character comedy beat. The speed of the reversal ('This is disgusting. I've got money coming in.') is what makes it. Complete economy of character revelation.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'She'll be the one driving the staircar from now on. It was part of the deal.' George Michael: 'You gave her my car?' Maeby: 'Yeah, it's technically hers anyways, 'cause of the Bluth Company.' George Michael: 'Oh.' 'But I did get you her 1988 yellow Cadillac.' 'Yeah?' 'She only uses it to and from hip replacements.' 'I guess that's only funny if you know who she is.'
jokes.setup: Maeby traded George Michael's staircar for Lucille 2's old Cadillac
jokes.punchline: 'She only uses it to and from hip replacements' — then George Michael's meta-acknowledgment: 'I guess that's only funny if you know who she is'
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke — 'I guess that's only funny if you know who she is' — is one of the most self-aware moments in the episode. George Michael's acknowledgment that the joke requires audience knowledge is brilliant AD meta-comedy. It works both for fans (who find it funny for that reason) and newcomers (who find the admission itself funny). One of the episode's strongest moments. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Pest control worker mentions he used to date a guy named Steve Holt. Maeby reacts. Worker: 'Ha! Steve Holt!' — doing Steve Holt's signature self-celebration.
jokes.setup: The pest control worker is making small talk about a former date
jokes.punchline: He spontaneously does the 'Steve Holt!' self-celebration — the recurring AD gag where people announce Steve Holt's name with arms raised
jokes.explanation: The Steve Holt callback is a genuine delight for AD fans. The reveal that the pest control worker dated Steve Holt, then his spontaneous 'Ha! Steve Holt!' celebration, is perfectly constructed. High rewatch and quotability value.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Although the house wouldn't remain pest-free for long. Heck of a birthday this is shaping up to be. In fact, it was about to get worse.'
jokes.setup: The pest control treatment has just been done
jokes.punchline: The narrator casually mentions it's someone's birthday while noting things are about to get worse — the cheerful 'Heck of a birthday' in a doom context is perfectly timed
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan 'Heck of a birthday' is a solid throwaway joke. The setup-and-immediate-subversion structure is efficient.

jokes.text: Michael and Gob arrive at the model home. Gob: 'I know, I've got these things very, you know, meticulously maintained.' Then: 'There are some vultures. They might still smell Pete.'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining why there are vultures circling
jokes.punchline: 'They might still smell Pete' — delivered casually, implying Pete is dead and has been there recently enough for vultures to smell
jokes.explanation: The casual delivery of 'they might still smell Pete' — implying a recent death/body — is classic AD dark comedy. Gob's complete lack of distress about Pete's fate is the joke.

jokes.text: Maeby reveals the Fakeblock office: a giant airplane hangar. 'When Fakeblock hits, we're going to have to ramp up real fast. This hangar can hold over 500 nerds!'
jokes.setup: George Michael is shown the Fakeblock office
jokes.punchline: It's a massive airplane hangar — completely disproportionate for a two-person operation with no product
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of renting a 500-person hangar for software that doesn't exist yet is peak Maeby overreach. '500 nerds' as a unit of measurement is a good touch.

jokes.text: Maeby hires Mort Meyers and makes him get her coffee: 'I'm a softie; I hired the guy. He's more like a mascot than anything. Mort, where's my coffee?' / 'Oh, damn. Oh, stupid Mort.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has set up a Fakeblock office and hired Mort.
jokes.punchline: The former studio executive is now a mascot fetching coffee, and he berates himself ('stupid Mort') with complete resignation.
jokes.explanation: Good character reversal — Mort's self-flagellation ('stupid Mort') is funny in its resigned specificity. The status reversal (studio boss to mascot coffee-fetcher) is a clean ironic beat.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Cinco de Quatro is in two days. But we'll be doing it on a boat, so I hope you don't get seasick.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is scheduling the Fakeblock launch
jokes.punchline: 'Cinco de Quatro' — the AD Season 4 invented holiday (May 4th, one day before Cinco de Mayo) — referenced here as a real event
jokes.explanation: A callback to the Season 4 invented holiday that ties the episode into the larger season arc. The matter-of-fact treatment of 'Cinco de Quatro' as a normal event is well-executed.

jokes.text: Mort shuffles in holding coffee: 'I got her coffee.' — after Maeby has just been fired by George Michael.
jokes.setup: Maeby was just fired; dramatic moment
jokes.punchline: Mort arrives holding coffee — completely late, completely irrelevant — as a coda to the drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing. The delayed coffee arrival as the scene's coda is exactly the kind of structural joke AD does brilliantly. The fact that Mort is now reduced to coffee-fetching makes it funnier. Economy is near-perfect — three words carry the whole joke.

jokes.text: Maeby gets Michael to sign a document for 'a movie about the family.' Michael: 'Is that still happening?' Maeby: 'What do you mean?' Michael: 'I got to tell you, I think movies are dead. Maybe it's a TV show.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is getting Michael to sign something
jokes.punchline: Michael's suggestion that 'movies are dead, maybe it's a TV show' is a meta-reference to the show's own revival on Netflix — Arrested Development itself being a TV show that came back
jokes.explanation: A genuinely sharp meta-joke. 'Movies are dead, maybe it's a TV show' refers to the show's own history — AD was cancelled as a TV show, then revived on Netflix (a streaming platform). The character saying this in-world makes it both self-referential and character-consistent for Michael. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Lindsay gives Maeby money from Herbert Love and mentions facial reconstruction: 'And if you choose to do facial reconstruction... I mean, the nose, right?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is returning money to Maeby with apparent maternal concern.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately uses the moment of apparent generosity to critique Maeby's nose — the insult delivered as incidental parenting observation.
jokes.explanation: Devastating character-specific beat — only Lindsay would pivot from returning money to suggesting rhinoplasty for her daughter. The 'I mean, the nose, right?' delivered as if seeking confirmation is perfectly calibrated casual cruelty. Very high character integration.

jokes.text: Perfecto punching Herbert Love — 'the little guy with the cigarette?' / 'No, the ripped black guy behind him.' / 'I just... I just didn't look up high enough.'
jokes.setup: Maeby points out Herbert Love for Perfecto to confront.
jokes.punchline: Perfecto focuses on the wrong (shorter) man first — 'I just didn't look up high enough' suggests he misjudged and went after the wrong target due to height.
jokes.explanation: Decent physical comedy beat. 'I just didn't look up high enough' is a good specific excuse for targeting the wrong person. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Perfecto: 'I'm a cop.' Maeby: 'But I saw your badge at the bar.' Perfecto: 'In fact, it was an anti-bullying badge. He'd received it from a police-run anti-bullying program at school. "You're never too old to stand up to a bully."'
jokes.setup: Throughout the episode, Perfecto has been believed to be an undercover cop
jokes.punchline: His 'badge' was an anti-bullying badge from a school program — he's just a 17-year-old who lied to impress Maeby
jokes.explanation: The anti-bullying badge reveal is one of the best structural jokes of the episode. An entire subplot has been built on a misread badge from a school anti-bullying program. The callback to 'You're never too old to stand up to a bully' (delivered earnestly now that it applies to a 17-year-old) is perfect. The irony that Maeby tried to entrap an actual child is deeply dark and funny. Strong 8.5+.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I'm 23-year-old woman. If anyone found out that we already had sex, it would be a major felony.'
jokes.setup: Maeby breaks up with Perfecto once she learns he's 17
jokes.punchline: She immediately calculates the legal exposure — she's committed statutory rape on an undercover... anti-bullying advocate
jokes.explanation: The reversal — Maeby was trying to entrap Perfecto as a predator and has instead become the predator — is a perfectly dark irony. Her matter-of-fact 'major felony' assessment is good character comedy.

jokes.text: 'But we messed around.' / 'Oh, they did more than that.' / 'Don't worry; nobody saw.' / 'Except Lucille 2.'
jokes.setup: Perfecto is reassuring Maeby about their encounter.
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms Lucille 2 saw — and this will have consequences. The 'except' construction is a reliable AD format but perfectly deployed.
jokes.explanation: Standard AD narrator correction format ('nobody saw — except...') but well-executed. Sets up further consequences economically.

jokes.text: I made up all that stuff to impress you; I'm just a senior. / A señor? / I'm 17. / Ha! / You're 17? / Yeah. / I'm 23. / Oh, that's a relief. / I wasn't supposed to be driving with you.
jokes.setup: Perfecto admits he's not a cop, just a senior — meaning a high school senior.
jokes.punchline: Maeby mishears "senior" as "señor" (Spanish for Mister/Sir) — then they compare ages: he's 17, she's 23. Her "relief" is that she's the adult, then immediately undercut by "I wasn't supposed to be driving with you."
jokes.explanation: A multi-layered wordplay and misdirection sequence. "Senior/señor" is a perfect homophone pun that works especially given Perfecto's heritage. Maeby's 'relief' at being the adult reverses immediately into a new problem (underage driving). The rapid escalation from relief to new jeopardy is brilliantly structured. The episode title 'Señoritis' itself is the punchline to this joke — it's wordplay on 'senioritis' and 'señor.' One of the best sequences in the episode.

jokes.text: Donny Richter: 'Congratulations. You got your first A.' Maeby: 'Oh, thank God. This A is finally gonna get me out of high school.' Richter: 'No, sex offender, your A is going to jail.'
jokes.setup: Maeby finally gets an A — a breakthrough after years in high school
jokes.punchline: The A is on the sex offender registry, not her transcript — the 'A' is a grade designation for sex offenders
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Maeby's entire arc has been about graduating high school, and her 'first A' is on the sex offender registry. The double meaning of 'A' is perfectly set up and the reversal is complete. 'Sex offender, your A is going to jail' as a line is structured as a teacher addressing a student — the genre flip (class to criminal designation) is seamless. This is a 9-range joke.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'You can't arrest me; you're an algebra teacher.' Richter: 'Donny Richter is an algebra teacher. I'm Rocky Richter. I told you there was an undercover cop in this school.'
jokes.setup: The episode-long mystery of the undercover cop at school
jokes.punchline: The undercover cop was the algebra teacher all along — Donny and Rocky Richter are different people; the 'there's an undercover cop' warning he gave Maeby earlier was self-referentially him warning her about himself
jokes.explanation: The episode's best structural joke and its primary payoff. 'Donny Richter is an algebra teacher. I'm Rocky Richter' is a devastating reveal because: (1) the undercover cop has been warning Maeby about himself the whole episode, (2) the twin-teacher twist recontextualizes every earlier scene, (3) 'I told you there was an undercover cop in this school' is an incredible callback to his own earlier warning. This is a textbook AD long-game joke that rewards patience. Rivals the 8.0-9.5 calibration examples. A strong 9+.

jokes.text: Final line: 'Oh, God. I'm gonna have to live in Sudden Valley.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has just been arrested (or is about to be) as a sex offender
jokes.punchline: 'Sudden Valley' — an AD callback to the failed Bluth real estate development that became a neighborhood of sex offenders; Maeby knows exactly where she'll end up
jokes.explanation: The perfect final line. 'Sudden Valley' is one of AD's most established running gags — the Bluth development that nobody bought except sex offenders. Maeby knowing she'll end up there is both a character beat (she's always ended up in her family's orbit) and a callback that rewards years of viewing. The economy is exceptional — five words close the episode with total clarity. One of the episode's top three jokes.

tmdb_overview: Maeby moves out on her own and reconnects with her cousin George-Michael.
insight: Wordplay-driven episode sustains 83.5 score across 76 jokes with minimal standout moments.
